did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a customized deep learning model, specifically an extended U-Net architecture with attention mechanisms, for skin cancer detection tasks including segmentation and classification of melanoma and non-melanoma images. The study reports performance metrics such as accuracy (97.99%) on the DermIS and DermQuest datasets and compares the model against other deep learning methods like SegNet, RU-Net, and R2U-Net.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Full Length Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work, including the design of a customized extended U-Net architecture, data collection from DermIS, DermQuest, and ISIC (2016) datasets, and the reporting of new performance metrics (e.g., 97.99% accuracy) compared against existing models.

title: Enhancing skin cancer detection with explainable artificial intelligence: customized extended deep U-shaped network approach
journal: Journal of King Saud University - Science
brief_summary: This study proposes a customized extended U-Net architecture with attention mechanisms to enhance skin cancer detection through simultaneous segmentation and classification of melanoma and non-melanoma lesions. The model was trained and evaluated using data from the DermIS, DermQuest, and ISIC (2016) datasets. Performance was assessed by comparing the proposed method against existing deep learning models such as SegNet, RU-Net, and R2U-Net. The extended U-Net achieved a classification accuracy of 97.99% on the DermIS and DermQuest datasets and 92.75% on the ISIC (2016) dataset. Additionally, the study incorporated Grad-CAM visual explanations to improve model interpretability and clinical decision-making support. The results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms several state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and segmentation precision.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two broad categories of skin cancer studied. The dataset consists of categorical images of melanoma and non-melanoma.

One of the two broad categories of skin cancer studied. Includes squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) and basal cell carcinoma (BCC) as mentioned in the introduction. The dataset consists of categorical images of melanoma and non-melanoma.

Mentioned in the introduction as a type of non-melanoma skin cancer, but not explicitly listed as a distinct class in the experimental classification task which used binary melanoma vs. non-melanoma labels.

Mentioned in the introduction as a type of non-melanoma skin cancer, but not explicitly listed as a distinct class in the experimental classification task which used binary melanoma vs. non-melanoma labels.

all_task_types.details: The model performs binary classification of skin cancer images into melanoma and non-melanoma classes. The paper reports classification accuracy metrics (97.99% on DermIS/DermQuest and 92.75% on ISIC 2016) and uses Softmax activation for multi-class classification logic, though the specific task is binary. The study explicitly states it extends beyond segmentation by including classification.

The model simultaneously performs segmentation of skin lesions using masks. Performance is evaluated using Intersection over Union (IoU) metrics. The architecture is an extended U-Net designed for both segmentation and classification.

datasets_used.name: DermIS and DermQuest
datasets_used.description: Publicly available dermatology image search (DermIS) and Dermatology quest (DermQuest) databases containing categorical skin cancer images with corresponding masks.

use.training: Used for training the customized extended U-Net model (144 samples).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the paper mentions evaluating performance over the test case sample and differences between train and test performance.
use.testing: Used for testing the model, achieving 97.99% accuracy on the image set and 97.51% on the mask set.
use.other: Used for visual explainability analysis and comparison with other models.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (2016)
datasets_used.description: International Skin Imaging Collaboration dataset, sourced from a public platform, used for external validation and comparison.

use.training: Used for training the model (900 samples).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split.
use.testing: Used for testing the model, achieving 92.75% accuracy on the image set and 91.75% on the mask set.
use.other: Used for external comparison against state-of-the-art methods.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, loss, and Intersection over Union (IoU) metrics, with comparisons made against other models based on these deterministic metrics rather than statistical significance testing.

DermIS and DermQuest.notes: The dataset consisted of 180 total samples (93 melanoma, 87 non-melanoma), split into 144 training and 36 testing samples. The proposed model outperformed existing methods. Loss, accuracy, and IoU curves were plotted for training analysis.

ISIC (2016).notes: The dataset consisted of 1279 total samples, split into 900 training and 379 testing samples. The model demonstrated robustness across this external dataset. Grad-CAM visual explanations were provided for this dataset.

architecture_details.structure: Extended U-Net with encoding-decoding blocks and attention gate mechanisms
architecture_details.encoder_blocks: Four encoding blocks (Encoder 1-4) with convolutional layers, ReLU activation, dropout (rate 0.5), and max pooling
architecture_details.attention_gates: Four attention gates (Attention 1-4) integrated before merging features in the decoder to emphasize important regions and suppress irrelevant details
architecture_details.decoder_blocks: Four decoder blocks (Decoder 1-4) using transpose convolutions (up-sampling) and concatenation with skip connections from encoder blocks
architecture_details.activation_functions: ReLU for intermediate layers, Softmax for multi-class classification logic, Sigmoid for final segmentation mask output

training_details.hardware: MacBook Pro (Retina, 13-inch, Mid 2014), 2.8 GHz Dual-Core Intel Core i5, 8 GB RAM, Intel Iris 1536 MB graphics
training_details.software: Anaconda Jupyter Notebook, Python 3.12.4, macOS Big Sur 11.7.10

classification_or_output_head.type: Multi-class classification logic via Softmax; Segmentation mask via Sigmoid
classification_or_output_head.details: The model performs simultaneous segmentation and classification. The final output layer generates a single-channel feature map for segmentation masks. Softmax activation is used to facilitate multi-class classification logic for melanoma vs. non-melanoma.

preprocessing_techniques: Image augmentation including flipping and rotations

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as a geometric transform to enhance model generalization during training.
Applied as a geometric transform to enhance model generalization during training.

conclusions: The proposed customized extended deep U-shaped network (U-Net) with attention mechanisms effectively enhances skin cancer detection by simultaneously performing segmentation and classification of melanoma and non-melanoma lesions.

The model achieved a classification accuracy of 97.99% on the DermIS and DermQuest datasets and 92.75% on the ISIC (2016) dataset, demonstrating high precision and robustness across different dermatological datasets.

The integration of attention gates in the extended U-Net architecture allows the model to emphasize important regions and suppress irrelevant details, leading to superior performance compared to existing models such as SegNet, RU-Net, R2U-Net, and various U-Net variants.

The inclusion of visual explainability methods, specifically Grad-CAM, provides interpretable visual explanations that support clinical decision-making by highlighting the regions of interest in the skin cancer images.

The study addresses the limitation of previous U-Net architectures that primarily focus on segmentation without incorporating classification or visual explainability, offering a comprehensive solution for automated skin cancer detection.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small custom dataset (180 images from DermIS/DermQuest) with a limited test set (36 images), raising concerns about statistical power and generalizability.

The ISIC (2016) dataset was used for both training (900 samples) and testing (379 samples) within the same experimental framework, which may constitute data leakage or at least prevents it from being a true external validation of the model trained on DermIS/DermQuest.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences against state-of-the-art models.
The model was trained on consumer-grade hardware (MacBook Pro, 8 GB RAM), which limits the reproducibility of training conditions for larger-scale deep learning applications.
The paper does not report performance metrics for specific subtypes of non-melanoma skin cancer (e.g., Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. Squamous Cell Carcinoma), limiting clinical specificity.
Data preprocessing involved organizing images in ascending order by index, which may introduce ordering bias if not properly randomized during the train-test split.
The study claims robustness based on performance on ISIC (2016), but since this dataset was part of the training process, it does not demonstrate true out-of-distribution generalization.
